original: Conjunction of Venus and Jupiter on August 12, 3 BC, a popular theory for the biblical "Star of Bethlehem"
The Battle of Ascalon is won by the Crusader army led by Godfrey of Bouillon against the Fatimid forces and is the last action of the First Crusade
Battle of Didgori: Georgian army under King David the Builder wins a decisive victory over the renowned Seljuk commander Ilghazi
Battle of Harim: Nur ad-Din defeats the Crusader armies of the County of Tripoli and the Principality of Antioch
Treaty of Nöteborg between Sweden and Novgorod (Russia) regulates the border for the first time
Battle of Dupplin Moor: Scottish dynastic conflict
Ottoman troops conquer Otranto in southern Italy after a 15-day siege, killing 12,000, enslaving 5,000, and beheading 800 Christians for refusing to convert to Islam
First engagement of the Battle of Zonchio between Venetian and Ottoman fleets
French troops conquer Ambleteuse in Pas-de-Calais and kill all the English prisoners
Commander of the English fleet, Lord Howard of Effingham, calls off the chase of the Spanish Armada off the coast of Scotland
Radboud Castle on Medemblik harbor in North Holland surrenders to the Spanish army
First American police force forms in New Amsterdam
First war between American colonists and Indigenous peoples ends in New England
Battle of Mohács: Ottoman invaders of Hungary are routed by forces of the Holy Roman Emperor under Charles of Lorraine
Battle of Kunersdorf: Russian-Austrian army overpowers Prussians
The Rhône department is created when the former department of Rhône-et-Loire is split into two: Rhône and Loire
Dutch troops conquer Louvain, Belgium
Netherlands and Belgium sign peace treaty
Chicago incorporates as a village, with a population of about 350 people
Anthony Faas of Philadelphia, Pennsylvania, is granted a second patent for improvements to the accordion (Patent No. 15511A)
In a skirmish in Texas, Apache Indians attack rebels
First cargo of lumber leaves Burrard Inlet, Vancouver, BC
Self-proclaimed Emperor Joshua Abraham Norton of the USA issues an edict abolishing the Democratic and Republican parties
Madeline (US) defeats Countess of Dufferin (Canada) in the fourth America's Cup
First National Archery Association tournament in Chicago
Yiddish theater opens in New York City with Abraham Goldfaden's operetta "Di Kishefmakhern" (The Sorceress)
The last quagga (a zebra subspecies with fewer stripes) dies at Artis Magistra Zoo in Amsterdam
Bill Murdoch scores the first Test cricket double century, 211, at The Oval
Willem II soccer team forms in Tilburg, Kingdom of the Netherlands
Hawaii is formally annexed to US
Boer General Kritzinger is driven out of Cape Colony
Japanese Minister to Russia presents a note to the Russian government protesting its failure to evacuate Manchuria
Cavalry battle in Halen, Belgium ("Battle of the Silver Helmets")
France and Great Britain declare war on Austria-Hungary
German 16.5"/12" guns fires on forts round Liege
WWI: Russian troops take East Prussia and occupy Marggrabowa
"Of Human Bondage" by William Somerset Maugham is published
WWI: Allies defeat Germans at the Battle of Amiens, the last great battle on the Western Front
Battle of Warsaw between Poland and Russia begins
Dutch AR leader Colijn replaces De Geer as Minister of Finance
Enrico Tiraboschi (36) of Argentina is the first to swim the English Channel from France to England
KMA-AM in Shenandoah, IA, begins radio transmissions
The first cast of Alpha Psi Omega, drawn from The Masquers of Fairmont College, West Virginia, is initiated
The tariff law comes into force in Germany, setting tariffs for industry and agriculture based on pre-war rates
IX Summer Olympic Games close at Olympisch Stadion in Amsterdam, Netherlands
Yangtze River floods after heavy rain crumbles dikes in China
Cuban dictator Machado y Morales flees following a military coup
120°F (49°C) in Seymour, Texas (state record)
After a 24-year absence, a baseball demonstration game is played between two American teams at the Berlin Olympics, with the World Champions beating the US Olympics 6-5
American diver Marjorie Gestring wins the 3 m springboard gold at the Berlin Olympics, becoming the youngest Olympic gold medalist at 13 years and 268 days
The gymnastics competition at the Berlin Olympics concludes with Germany dominating, winning six of nine gold medals, including the men's and women's team events
Sabotage is suspected in the City of San Francisco train crash, which derails near Harney, Nevada, killing 24
Luftwaffe bombs British radar stations and loses 31 aircraft
Netherlands starts rationing textiles
German forces capture Elista, Kalmyk Steppe
Alleged date of the first Philadelphia Experiment test on United States Navy ship USS Eldridge
Pipeline under the ocean (Pluto) begins operating
Waffen SS troops massacre 560 people in Sant'Anna di Stazzema
Cleveland Indians get 29 hits in a 9-inning game
Court of Justice sentences General Friedrich Christiansen, commander of the German Wehrmacht in the Netherlands, to 12 years' imprisonment
Indian maestro Balbir Singh Dosanjh scores twice as India beats Great Britain 4-0 for the field hockey gold medal at the London Olympics, India's fourth consecutive Olympic hockey title
The canoeing program at the London Olympics concludes with Sweden (4) and Czechoslovakia (3) dominating, combining for 7 of the 9 gold medals
16th NFL Chicago All-Star Game: Philadelphia 38, All-Stars 0 (93,780 attendees)
First international game by an NFL team, New York Giants beat CFL's Ottawa Rough Riders 20-6 at Ottawa's Lansdowne Stadium
A 6.8 earthquake strikes the Ionian Islands, killing between 445 and 800 people
Ann Davison arrives in Miami in her 23-foot boat Felicity Ann, becoming the first woman to sail solo across the Atlantic
Soviet Union conducts a secret test of its first hydrogen bomb
Washington Senators' Eddie Yost draws his 100th walk for the fifth consecutive year
22nd NFL Chicago All-Star Game: All-Stars 30, Cleveland 27 (75,000 attendees)
Hurricane Connie makes landfall near Fort Macon, North Carolina, as a Category 3 storm
WPBT TV channel 2 in Miami, FL (PBS) begins broadcasting
KOTI TV channel 2 in Klamath Falls, OR (NBC/CBS) begins broadcasting

Art Kane photographs a group portrait of 57 leading jazz musicians assembled in front of a brownstone on 126th Street in NYC for Esquire magazine; originally titled "Harlem, 1958," it appears as the centerfold of Esquire's January 1959 issue celebrating the "Golden Age of Jazz"
First ship firing of a Polaris missile from Observation Island
Progressive Party under John Steytler forms in South Africa
27th NFL Chicago All-Star Game: Baltimore 32, All-Stars 7 (70,000 attendees)
Echo 1, the first communications satellite, is launched by NASA
USAF Major Robert M. White takes the X-15 to an altitude of 41,600 meters
Russia launches Vostok 4 with Pavel Popovich, who lands safely on August 15
Portuguese dictator Salazar is firm on African colonization
Race riot in Elizabeth, New Jersey
Dame Elizabeth Lane becomes the first female English High Court judge
Milwaukee Brewers Baseball Club, Inc. applies for an NL franchise
Race riot on the West Side of Chicago
Longview, Texas, radio station KLUE holds a "Beatles Bonfire" to burn Beatles records and memorabilia; the station is struck by lightning the following day
The last Beatles concert tour of North America begins with two shows at the International Amphitheatre in Chicago, Illinois
New Orleans Saints' first pre-season victory, beating St. Louis 23-14

Battle of the Bogside: RUC officers, backed by loyalists, enter the nationalist Bogside in armored cars and try to suppress the riot by using CS gas, water cannon, and eventually firearms; the almost continuous rioting lasts for two days
Boston Celtics is sold for an NBA record $6 million
Last American combat ground troops leave Vietnam
Betty Morris wins WPBA National Championship
YankeesMickey Mantleand Whitey Ford become the first teammates inducted into the Hall of Fame on the same day
Christian militia conquers the Palestinian camp of Tel al-Zaatar, killing 2,000 people
First Approach and Landing Test (ALT) of Orbiter Enterprise
For the second consecutive day, Oakland's Manny Sanguillen foils a no-hit bid
High Energy Astronomy Observatory 1 (HEAO) is launched into Earth's orbit by NASA
Space Shuttle Enterprise makes its first atmospheric test flight
China and Japan sign a peace treaty
International Cometary Explorer (ICE) spacecraft launches to study solar wind
NFL New England Patriots wide receiver Darryl Stingley suffers a spinal cord injury, leaving him with incomplete quadriplegia after a hit by Oakland Raiders' Jack Tatum in a preseason exhibition game
Iranian press censors start massive book burnings
Nicaragua celebrates the end of its National Literacy Crusade, a massive effort over five months that reduces illiteracy from 50% to 12%
Signing of the Montevideo Treaty establishing the Latin American Integration Association
American swimmer Jon Erikson becomes the first to complete a three-way crossing of the English Channel in 38 hours and 27 minutes
Braves beat Padres 5-3, featuring 2 brawls and 19 ejections
Carlos Lopes of Portugal wins the men's marathon at the Los Angeles Olympics in 2:09:21, an Olympic record that stands for 24 years
XXIII Summer Olympic Games close at the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum
520 people die when Japan Airlines Flight 123 crashes in Ueno, Japan, the second-deadliest aviation disaster of all time
Baltimore Orioles' W. Gross and L. Sheets are the sixth to hit consecutive pinch home runs
Boston Red Sox outfielder Don Baylor gets hit by a pitch for a record 25th time in a season
Iran fires a missile at a refinery near Baghdad; Iraq raids an Iranian terminal at Sirri Island, severely disrupting Iranian exports
Red Sox pitcher Tim Lollar gets a pinch-hit single
Charles Cole climbs the 870-foot Tyrolean Traverse from the top of Elephant Rock
Boston Red Sox beat Tigers 9-4 for an AL record 23rd consecutive win at home
Movie "The Last Temptation of Christ" is released
Richard Thornburgh becomes US Attorney General
12th Annual Macy's Tap-o-mania
Longest baseball rain delay in MLB history as the Texas Rangers and Chicago White Sox wait 7 hours and 23 minutes at Comiskey Park in Chicago until the game is eventually postponed
Creditors vote to support Greyhound Bus reorganization plan
Canada, Mexico, and the United States announce completion of negotiations for the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA)
First NFL game on Fox Network (exhibition - SF vs. Denver)
Panic in the Church of Brazzaville, Congo, due to a sudden storm; 142 killed
Stephen G. Breyer is sworn in as a Supreme Court Justice
"Breakfast on Fox" premieres
The Oscar class submarine K-141 Kursk of the Russian Navy explodes and sinks in the Barents Sea during a military exercise
New Jersey Governor James McGreevey comes out publicly as gay
An F1 tornado strikes Glen Cove, New York, a rare event on Long Island
An F2 tornado strikes the coal mining town of Wright, Wyoming, destroying nearly 100 homes and killing two people
Civil unrest is provoked in the Maldives
Sri Lanka's foreign minister, Lakshman Kadirgamar, is fatally shot by a Liberation Tigers of Tamil Eelam sniper at his home
Bulk carrier M/V New Flame collides with oil tanker Torm Gertrud at the southernmost tip of Gibraltar and ends up partially submerged
American swimmer Natalie Coughlin becomes the first to successfully defend the gold medal in the women's 100 m backstroke when she wins the event at the Beijing Olympics
XXX Summer Olympic Games close at Olympic Stadium at Queen Elizabeth Olympic Park, London, England
Eight people are killed and 25 are injured in a suicide bomb attack in Balad, Iraq
Ibrahim Boubacar Keïta is elected President of Mali
Thirty people are killed by gunmen in northeastern Nigeria
Ebola outbreak death toll exceeds 1000
World Health Organization (WHO) gives its 'cautious blessing' to the use of experimental drugs to treat the Ebola virus
China cuts its currency, the yuan, for the second day in a row to boost exports
In London, archaeologists discover a mass grave of 30 bodies from the 1665 plague
Large series of explosions in Tianjin, China, leave at least 50 dead and 700 injured in an industrial accident
Cannes becomes the first French resort to ban the burkini
Hungarian swimmer Katinka Hosszú wins her fourth medal of the Rio de Janeiro Olympics, finishing second in the women's 200 m backstroke; gold goes to American Maya DiRado in 2:05.99
Italian jockey Frankie Dettori, aboard Predilection, rides his 3,000th British winner at Newmarket

Joseph Schooling of Singapore sets an Olympic record of 50.39 to win the men's 100 m butterfly gold at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics; a rare three-way tie for silver at 51.14 involvesMichael Phelps(USA), Chad le Clos (South Africa), and László Cseh (Hungary)
"Unite the Right" march in Charlottesville, Virginia turns violent when a car rams protesters, killing one and injuring 19
Mali's presidential run-off election is held, and current President Ibrahim Boubacar Keita eventually wins, with the opposition disputing the outcome
More than 200 Afghan soldiers are reported killed after three days of attacks by Taliban insurgents over multiple fronts, including Ghazni City
NASA launches the Parker Solar Probe, its first mission to the Sun and its outermost atmosphere, the corona
Baltimore Orioles concede 7 home runs in doubleheader defeats against NY Yankees to break the American League single-season HRs allowed record with 248 and counting
Beginning of the "Glitter Revolution" in Mexico City when the security chief is showered with pink glitter in protest of violence against women after a teenager is raped by police
Conservative Alejandro Giammattei is elected president of Guatemala
Hong Kong International Airport cancels all departures as thousands of anti-government protesters occupy the terminals
Scientists say they are closer to an effective treatment for Ebola after a new drug trial has a 90% success rate in the Democratic Republic of the Congo
Europe fights a new COVID-19 surge with Germany, France, and Spain posting their largest daily infection totals for three months
UK posts its worst quarterly economic slump on record, -20.4% (Apr-Jun), pushing it into the largest recession globally
Yemeni authorities say at least 172 people died in floods caused by torrential rains, which also destroy historic buildings in Sanaa, a UNESCO World Heritage Site
195 million Americans across 34 states are under heat advisory warnings from the Pacific Northwest to the Northeast as the summer of intense heat continues in the Northern Hemisphere
Bennu Asteroid, the size of the Empire State Building, now has a 1-in-1,750 chance of hitting Earth in 2182 according to data from NASA's OSIRIS-REx spacecraft
Britney Spears' father says he will step down as her conservator after the singer calls his conservatorship abusive amid a #FreeBritney campaign
Phoenix is the US's fastest-growing city (11.2%), overtaking Philadelphia to be the nation's fifth largest at 1.6 million according to the US Census
US Census reveals country grows more diverse, with growth in Hispanic (+23%) and Asian (+36%) populations, and the first-ever decline in white population (-2.6%)
US FDA authorizes a third COVID-19 vaccine booster shot for people with weakened immune systems
Drought officially declared in eight regions of the UK amid the country's driest period since 1976
Bones of a British polar researcher who died in Antarctica in 1959 are discovered in a melting glacier; he is formally recognized as Dennis Bell
US President Donald Trump deploys at least 800 National Guard troops in Washington, DC, and declares federal control of the DC police force
English KingEdward IIIbans wool export to Flanders and later grants a company of merchants a monopoly on selling wool in attempt to maximise taxes
Christopher Columbusarrives in the Canary Islands on his first voyage to the New World
PopeJulius IIIorders the confiscation and burning of the Jewish Talmud, one of the main sources of Jewish law and theology
Duke of Wellington's Anglo-Portuguese troops enter Madrid, Spain, following their victory at the Battle of Salamanca
Robert Southey is appointed British Poet Laureate by KingGeorge III
American inventorIsaac Singerpatents his famous sewing machine
Confederate GeneralJohn Hunt Morganand his raiders capture Gallatin, Texas
British surgeonJoseph Listerperforms the first antiseptic surgery using carbolic spray on instruments and bandages
US PresidentAndrew Johnsondefies Congress by suspending Secretary of WarEdwin Stanton
Bertha, wife of inventorKarl Benz, makes first motor tour
KingLeopold IIopens Antwerp Central Station in Belgium
Dedication ofFrederick Douglass' home in Washington, D.C. as a national shrine
Silent film "Wings," starringClara Bowand directed by William A. Wellman, premieres in NY as the first film and one of only two silent films to win an Academy Award for Best Picture
Clarence Birdseyeis granted a patent for a method for quick freezing food (patent US 1773079 A)
French MarshalPhilippe Pétaingives full support to Nazi Germany
British Prime MinisterWinston Churchillarrives in Moscow for a conference withJoseph Stalinand US representative W. Averell Harriman
Lt GeneralBernard Montgomeryarrives in Cairo
Winston ChurchillandTitomeet in Naples
Emperor Hirohitoof Japan informs the imperial family that he has decided to surrender
PopePius XIIpublishes encyclical Humani generis
US PresidentEisenhowerraises minimum wage from 75 cents to $1 an hour
AmericanRalph Bostonsets a long jump world record of 8.21 m at the US Olympic trials, surpassing Jesse Owens' record
Stan Musialannounces he will retire at end of year
Mickey Mantleswitch-hits a home run for the record 10th and final time in a game, with one going 502 feet
The Beatles hold a press conference at the Astor Tower Hotel, in Chicago, Illinois;John Lennonapologizes for his "more popular than Jesus" remark
Curt Floodloses his $4.1 million antitrust suit against baseball
Syrian PresidentHafez al-Assadbreaks off diplomatic relations with Jordan
Nolan Ryanstrikes out 19 and walks only 2 as Angels top Red Sox 4-2

Warner Bros. Records releases "One-Trick Pony," the fifth solo studio album by American singer-songwriterPaul Simon; it features the song "Late in the Evening" and is accompanied by a dramatic film of the same name, written by and starring Simon
Don Estridgeunveils the company's first personal computer, the IBM PC, at New York’s Waldorf Astoria Hotel, priced at $1,565 with 16 kilobytes of RAM, helping bring computing to the masses
GeneralManuel Noriegabecomes commander of Panamanian army
Harmon Killebrew,Rick Ferrell,Don Drysdale, Pee Wee Reese, and Luis Aparicio are inducted into the Baseball Hall of Fame in Cooperstown, New York
Nelson Mandelais treated for tuberculosis at a Tygerberg Hospital in Cape Town, South Africa
Iraqi PresidentSaddam Husseinsays he is ready to resolve the Gulf crisis if Israel withdraws from occupied territories
Heavy metal bandMetallicareleases its fifth album, "Metallica," which debuts at #1 on the Billboard 200 chart
PopeJohn Paul IIbegins a visit to the US
Shakirarecords her first live album in New York City, titled "MTV Unplugged"
Lee Hsien Loongsucceeds Goh Chok Tong as Prime Minister of Singapore
American super-swimmerMichael Phelpswins his third of eight gold medals at the Beijing Olympics when he wins the 200 m freestyle in a world record time of 1:42.96
An American 1-2 in the 100 m backstroke at the Beijing Olympics withAaron Peirsolswimming a world record of 52.54 to defeat teammate Matt Grevers
Russo-Georgian War:Nicolas Sarkozy, the President of France, negotiates ceasefire agreement between Russia and Georgia, ending a brief war after 5 days of hostilities
Whitey Bulger, an American organized crime boss, is found guilty on 31 of 32 racketeering and firearms counts and is found to have been involved in 11 murders
Former US PresidentJimmy Carterreveals that he has cancer
US Postal Service issues a second postage stamp featuring rock singerElvis Presleyas part of its Music Icon series; the design is a black and white photograph taken by William Speer in 1955

American star swimmerKatie Ledeckysets a world record of 8:04.79 to dominate the women's 800 m field at the Rio de Janeiro Olympics; a medal tally of 4 golds and 1 silver makes her the most decorated US female athlete at one Olympics
US PresidentDonald Trump, at a press conference at Trump Tower, says “there is blame on both sides” in reference to violence in Charlottesville, provoking widespread condemnation
Usain Boltinjures himself in his final race, the men's 4 x 100 m relay; Jamaica misses gold at the World Championships
Kawhi Leonardre-signs with the Los Angeles Clippers to a max four-year, $176.3 million contract
British authorSalman Rushdieis attacked and repeatedly stabbed on stage before giving a lecture at Chautauqua Institution in Chautauqua, New York
Ai Moriwins a gold medal in lead climbing at the IFSC World Championships in Bern, Switzerland
To his amazement,Thomas Edisonrecords himself reciting "Mary Had a Little Lamb" on his newly completed cylinder phonograph, a device that records sound onto tinfoil cylinders
Peace protocol ending the Spanish–American War is signed
Ford Motor Company builds its first Model T car, whichHenry Fordhimself tests on a hunting trip to Wisconsin and northern Michigan
Pablo Picasso, Max Jacob, Moïse Kisling, Ortiz, and Paquerette are photographed in Paris
Prince Moriyoshi, 1st Japanese shōgun of the Kenmu Restoration (1333) and monk, born in Kamakura, Japan (d. 1335)
Abraham Zacuto, Spanish Jewish astronomer, mathematician and historian, whose ideas and astrolabe aided Vasco da Gama's voyages, born in Salamanca, Spain (d. 1515?)
Isabella Clara Eugenia, Sovereign of the Netherlands (1598-1621), born in Palacio de Valsaín, Segovia, Spain (d. 1633)
William Count of Nassau-Siegen, Dutch Field Marshall (Governor of Emmerich, Heusden and Sluis), born in Dillenburg (d. 1642)
Tokugawa Iemitsu, 3rd Japanese shōgun of the Tokugawa shogunate (1623-51) who expelled all Europeans from Japan and closed Japan's borders, born in Edo, Japan  (d. 1651)
Giovanni Legrenzi, Venetian Baroque organist and composer, born in Clusone, Republic of Venice (d. 1690)
Georg Christoph Leuttner, German ecclesiastical composer, born in Tölz, Duchy of Bavaria (d. 1703)

John Balguy, English clergyman and moral philosopher, born in Sheffield, England (d. 1748)

Jonas Hanway, English merchant, traveler, philanthropist and noted opponent of drinking tea, born in Portsmouth, England (d. 1786)

Rowland Hill, English preacher who advocated for the smallpox vaccine, born in Hawkstone Park, England (d. 1833)
Johann Baptist Lasser, German composer, born in Steinkirchen, Electorate of Bavaria (d. 1805)

Tedd Pierce, American cartoon screenwriter and voice actor (Warner Brothers), born in Quogue, New York (d. 1972)
Gladys Bentley, American blues singer, performer, and drag king pioneer during the Harlem Renaissance, born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1960)[1]
Joe Besser, American actor and comedian (The Three Stooges, Let's Make Love, Africa Screams), born in St. Louis, Missouri (d. 1988)

Heinrich Sutermeister, Swiss opera composer (Romeo und Julia; Raskolnikov; Das Flaschenteufel), born in Feuerthalen, Switzerland (d. 1995)
Norah Phillips, Baroness Phillips, British politician (1st female Whip - UK House of Lords), born in London, England (d. 1992)
Yusof bin Ishak, Malay first President of Singapore (1965-70), born in Trong, Perak, British Malaya (d. 1970)

Samuel Fuller, American screenwriter and director (Shock Corridor, The Big Red One), born in Worcester, Massachusetts (d. 1997)

Jan Bakker, Dutch WWII resistance fighter and journalist (Parool), born in Giethoorn, Netherlands (d. 1994)
Ruth Lowe, Canadian pianist and composer ("I'll Never Smile Again"), born in Toronto, Ontario (d. 1981)
